event: Archaeological evidence from caves in nearby Borneo, including the Niah Caves region, indicates human habitation in the area tens of thousands of years ago.
Austronesian-speaking peoples migrate into coastal Borneo, establishing the ancestral populations of the region's later Malay and indigenous communities.
Coastal trading settlements develop along northwestern Borneo, drawn into maritime networks linking China, India, and Southeast Asia.
Chinese records mention a kingdom called Poli, often associated with early Brunei, sending an embassy to the Chinese court.
Chinese Song dynasty records note a trade mission from Po-ni (Boni), an early Bruneian polity, marking some of the first firm references to the state.
Po-ni sends another tribute mission to China, reflecting sustained commercial and diplomatic ties with the Song dynasty.
Chinese official Zhao Rugua describes Po-ni as a prosperous trading state in his account of foreign lands.
Boni sends a tribute mission to the Ming dynasty as China reasserts its presence in maritime Southeast Asian trade.
King Abdul Majid Hassan of Boni dies during a state visit to Nanjing and is buried in China with full honors.
Awang Alak Betatar, traditionally regarded as the first sultan Muhammad Shah, converts to Islam and founds the Bruneian Sultanate as a Muslim state.
Sultan Bolkiah ascends the throne, beginning a reign that expands Brunei into a powerful maritime empire across northern Borneo and into the Philippines.
Under Sultan Bolkiah, the Bruneian Empire reaches its golden age, controlling coastal Borneo, Sulu, and parts of the Philippine archipelago including Manila.
Survivors of Ferdinand Magellan's expedition, including chronicler Antonio Pigafetta, visit Brunei and record its wealthy and well-organized court.
Spanish forces from the Philippines attack and briefly occupy Brunei during the Castilian War before being driven out by disease and resistance.
A civil war over the succession weakens the sultanate and accelerates the gradual decline of Bruneian power in the region.
Brunei's influence wanes as European trading powers and regional rivals erode its control over Borneo's coasts and trade routes.
British adventurer James Brooke arrives in Borneo and aids the sultanate in suppressing a rebellion in the Sarawak region.
Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddin II grants James Brooke governorship of Sarawak, making him the first White Rajah and beginning the dismemberment of Brunei's territory.
Brunei cedes the island of Labuan to Britain, which establishes a colony and naval coaling station there.
Brunei grants land concessions in northern Borneo to foreign interests, foreshadowing the later loss of Sabah.
Brunei becomes a British protectorate, retaining internal self-rule while Britain controls its foreign affairs and defense.
Sarawak under the Brooke regime annexes the Limbang district, splitting Brunei into two separate non-contiguous parts.
A British Resident is installed in Brunei, giving the British significant control over administration while preserving the sultan's nominal authority.
Oil is discovered at Seria, beginning the transformation of Brunei's economy into one based on petroleum wealth.
Japanese forces invade and occupy Brunei during World War II, seizing its oil fields and ending British administration.
Allied forces, primarily Australian troops, liberate Brunei from Japanese occupation near the end of World War II.
Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien III ascends the throne and begins modernizing the country using growing oil revenues.
Brunei adopts its first written constitution, establishing internal self-government while Britain retains responsibility for defense and foreign affairs.
The Brunei Revolt led by A.M. Azahari and the People's Party erupts against plans to join Malaysia and is suppressed with British military help.
Brunei declines to join the newly formed Federation of Malaysia, choosing instead to remain a separate British-protected state.
Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien III abdicates in favor of his son Hassanal Bolkiah, who becomes the 29th Sultan of Brunei.
Hassanal Bolkiah is formally crowned Sultan of Brunei in a lavish coronation ceremony.
Brunei and Britain sign a treaty of friendship and cooperation setting a timetable for full independence.
Brunei gains full independence from Britain on 1 January and that year joins ASEAN, the United Nations, and the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation.
The government promotes the national ideology of Melayu Islam Beraja, the Malay Islamic Monarchy, as the foundation of the state.
Brunei introduces stricter Islamic social policies, including restrictions on alcohol sales, reinforcing conservative national values.
Prince Al-Muhtadee Billah is proclaimed Crown Prince and heir apparent to the throne.
A high-profile legal dispute erupts over the alleged misuse of state funds by Prince Jefri, the sultan's brother and former finance minister.
Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah reconvenes the Legislative Council for the first time in two decades as a step toward limited constitutional reform.
Brunei begins phasing in a strict Sharia-based penal code, becoming the first East Asian country to adopt such a code at the national level.
Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah celebrates his Golden Jubilee marking fifty years on the throne.
Brunei implements harsh Sharia penalties including death by stoning, drawing international condemnation before pledging a moratorium on executions.
Brunei records its first cases of COVID-19 and imposes public health measures while continuing efforts to diversify its oil-dependent economy.
Brunei continues pursuing economic diversification under its Wawasan 2035 vision while remaining one of the world's last absolute monarchies.
